text: hummed to life, its interior chamber lighting up . “Please remove your clothing and enter the Epidermal Synthesizer,” the bot said as the chamber door slid open . Ronin stood his rifle against the wall, settled his pack on the floor beside it, and unfasted the buttons on his coat, draping it over one of the chairs. He added his shirt a moment later, and sat down to untie his boots. He kicked them off and slid them beneath the chair . The quiet clicking of the attendant bot’s fingers on the console was the only sound apart from the whisper of cloth as Ronin removed his pants, folded them over his arm, and added them to the pile . He shifted his optics down. His casing was visible through the holes on his abdomen, gleaming in the light cast by the synthesizer. Random patches of his skin were lighter or darker than the rest, result of dozens of repair jobs in dozens of towns . Though the discolored spots weren’t distinguishable from the rest of his skin in any other way — as smooth to the touch as everywhere else — they reminded him of the scars many organics bore. A subtle, visible history wrought in flesh. He could recall the circumstances behind each damage, no matter how minor, he’d ever suffered . “Please, enter the Epidermal Synthesizer,” the attendant repeated. Its faintly glowing optics were trained on Ronin, and though its face was incapable of expression, it seemed to hesitate. “Have you suffered damage deeper than the surface, Ronin ?” “I’m fine,” Ronin heard himself reply. He forced himself to walk to the chamber. As he entered, he assessed data, pondering the problem awaiting him at his residence, just over a mile away . Damage deeper than the surface . All bots had lost something in the Black Out. Parts of themselves that couldn’t be replaced. It was the sort of damage that couldn’t be repaired at a place like the Clinic, the sort of damage that could wreak subtle havoc on a bot’s core programming . Wasn’t that similar to the mental fragility of humans? Ronin had seen it firsthand in the Dust; humans, especially in stressful situations, often shattered . What hidden damage had Lara suffered? What internal scars did she carry ? The chamber door closed, sealing Ronin inside. It was utterly silent for several seconds. Then the synthesizer’s motors engaged, moving the scanning arms on either side of him. Their lights flickered on — blue, a welcome change from the harsh white that dominated the place, and a reminder of Lara’s eyes — and the arms circled Ronin, bathing him in their glow . Bots and humans were products of the Creators. Their differences were many, but why did synths, when fully repaired and maintained, look so much like humans? Was it simply an aesthetic that appealed to the eye of their makers? Perhaps they weren’t quite as different beneath their appearances as everyone thought . The scanners retracted, replaced by numerous fabrication arms. The synthesizer shifted to full operation, emitting a high whine. The small arms soldered delicate wires into the neural network on his casing, while the large ones, moving slowly, knitted synthetic skin over the top of the wiring . Though perfection was impossible, synths seemed the closest to it of all intelligent beings. Why, then, was a human so interesting to him? Why were her flaws more alluring, her imperfections more natural ? He closed his eyes, reducing his vision to the faint, brown-red glow that filtered through his eyelids. His true programming seemed simultaneously so close and so terribly far away. Pushing aside the various sensory inputs, he delved into his memory core, searching for the missing piece that would unlock what he’d lost . When he stumbled upon the memory of a shack on the edge of the road and the redhaired woman dancing within, he stopped . And played it over and over again . Chapter Ten T he afternoon dragged for Lara. She was accustomed to labor from dawn to dusk on most days; this idleness was torture . She took another hot shower, scrubbing her hair, body, and teeth. Then she stood — despite Ronin’s talk about not being wasteful — in the falling water for at least fifteen minutes, letting it melt away her aches . After stepping out of the tub and drying off, she dressed in her own clothes. Their smell alone was a comfort, reminding her of her old life; it hadn’t been good, but it was familiar, and that grounded her. She didn’t know if she’d ever feel like she belonged in Ronin’s world . Returning to her room, she placed the shirt Ronin gave her in the dresser’s top drawer, wondering if he’d been joking about people filling these things up with clothing. Why would someone need so many clothes ? She explored the other upstairs rooms. They were similar to hers in appearance —bare of furniture apart from beds and dressers, no decorations to be seen. The room closest to hers had pale blue walls instead of the white that covered the rest of the house. Still, the little differences, like the way her bedding was crumpled and disturbed and the collection of trinkets atop the dresser, made hers feel inviting . She stopped in the doorway of Ronin’s room, staring inside, oddly hesitant to enter. If he didn’t want her to go in, he’d have closed the door. It wasn’t like he cared about privacy, right ? Stepping across the threshold, she swept her gaze over the room. His bed was perfectly made, with squared corners and not so much as a wrinkle on the blanket. That made sense; Ronin didn’t sleep . What did he do to keep himself occupied? Did he just stare blankly at the walls? Did he think of … No. Not going there right now . After the space he’d given her
